Section 9 (Concluding remarks), page 33; also discussed after Theorem 2 in Section 4 (pages 15–17).

Similarly, strong duality is only established for convex \(h\) and affine cost, and we wonder whether it also holds in more general situations.
title: Strong duality for distorted optimal transport beyond affine costs and convex distortions
background_latex:
Let \((\Omega,\mathcal A,\mathbb P)\) be an atomless probability space and let \(\mathcal X\) be the set of bounded real-valued random variables.

A distortion function is an increasing function \(h:[0,1]\to[0,1]\) with \(h(0)=0\) and \(h(1)=1\). The distorted expectation \(\mathbb E_h:\mathcal X\to\mathbb R\) associated with \(h\) is the law-invariant functional defined by
\[\mathbb E_h[Z]=\int_0^{\infty} h(\mathbb P(Z>x))\,dx+\int_{-\infty}^0 \big(h(\mathbb P(Z>x))-1\big)\,dx,\qquad Z\in\mathcal X.\]

Fix Borel probability measures \(\mu,\nu\) on \(\mathbb R\). A coupling of \(\mu\) and \(\nu\) is a pair \((X,Y)\) of random variables with \(X\sim\mu\) and \(Y\sim\nu\).

Given a measurable cost function \(c:\mathbb R^2\to\mathbb R\), the distorted optimal transport problem considered in the paper is
\[\inf\{\mathbb E_h[c(X,Y)] : X\sim\mu,\ Y\sim\nu\}.\]

Define
\[S_c:=\{(\varphi,\psi):\ \varphi(x)+\psi(y)\le c(x,y)\ \text{for all }x,y\in\mathbb R\},\]
and let \(X_\mu\sim\mu\) and \(X_\nu\sim\nu\) be random variables with laws \(\mu\) and \(\nu\), respectively.

The paper establishes: (i) weak duality for a class of non-linear expectations; and (ii) strong duality in the special case where \(\mathbb E=\mathbb E_h\) and \(c\) is affine, i.e., \(c(x,y)=\alpha+\beta x+\gamma y\).
problem_definition_latex:
Determine conditions on a distortion function \(h\) and a cost function \(c:\mathbb R^2\to\mathbb R\) (beyond the affine-cost case) under which the strong duality identity
\[\inf_{X\sim\mu,\,Y\sim\nu}\mathbb E_h[c(X,Y)] 
= \sup_{(\varphi,\psi)\in S_c}\big\{\mathbb E_h[\varphi(X_\mu)]+\mathbb E_h[\psi(X_\nu)]\big\}
\]
holds (for given marginals \(\mu,\nu\)).

Let \((\Omega,\mathcal A,\mathbb P)\) be an atomless probability space and let \(\mathcal X\) be the set of bounded real-valued random variables.

A distortion function is an increasing function \(h:[0,1]\to[0,1]\) with \(h(0)=0\) and \(h(1)=1\). The distorted expectation \(\mathbb E_h:\mathcal X\to\mathbb R\) associated with \(h\) is the law-invariant functional defined by
\[\mathbb E_h[Z]=\int_0^{\infty} h(\mathbb P(Z>x))\,dx+\int_{-\infty}^0 \big(h(\mathbb P(Z>x))-1\big)\,dx,\qquad Z\in\mathcal X.\]

Fix Borel probability measures \(\mu,\nu\) on \(\mathbb R\). A coupling of \(\mu\) and \(\nu\) is a pair \((X,Y)\) of random variables with \(X\sim\mu\) and \(Y\sim\nu\).

Given a measurable cost function \(c:\mathbb R^2\to\mathbb R\), the distorted optimal transport problem considered in the paper is
\[\inf\{\mathbb E_h[c(X,Y)] : X\sim\mu,\ Y\sim\nu\}.\]

Define
\[S_c:=\{(\varphi,\psi):\ \varphi(x)+\psi(y)\le c(x,y)\ \text{for all }x,y\in\mathbb R\},\]
and let \(X_\mu\sim\mu\) and \(X_\nu\sim\nu\) be random variables with laws \(\mu\) and \(\nu\), respectively.

The paper establishes: (i) weak duality for a class of non-linear expectations; and (ii) strong duality in the special case where \(\mathbb E=\mathbb E_h\) and \(c\) is affine, i.e., \(c(x,y)=\alpha+\beta x+\gamma y\).

Determine conditions on a distortion function \(h\) and a cost function \(c:\mathbb R^2\to\mathbb R\) (beyond the affine-cost case) under which the strong duality identity
\[\inf_{X\sim\mu,\,Y\sim\nu}\mathbb E_h[c(X,Y)] 
= \sup_{(\varphi,\psi)\in S_c}\big\{\mathbb E_h[\varphi(X_\mu)]+\mathbb E_h[\psi(X_\nu)]\big\}
\]
holds (for given marginals \(\mu,\nu\)).
location_in_paper: Section 9 (Concluding remarks), page 33; also discussed after Theorem 2 in Section 4 (pages 15–17).
open_problem_quote: Similarly, strong duality is only established for convex \(h\) and affine cost, and we wonder whether it also holds in more general situations.
subject_classification: Optimal transport with non-linear expectations; duality theory
counterexample_suitability_rationale: Potential failures of strong duality could be probed by searching over simple discrete marginals and low-complexity costs and testing primal-versus-dual gaps numerically. However, the dual problem involves optimization over functions \((\varphi,\psi)\) with pointwise constraints, so making the search exhaustive or certified is nontrivial without restricting to finite supports and finite-dimensional parametrizations.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: While primal values can be estimated by optimizing over couplings on finite supports, reliably evaluating the dual supremum requires solving a potentially large constrained optimization over function pairs, and there is no straightforward, general automatic fitness signal for candidate theoretical conditions guaranteeing equality. Substantial mathematical guidance or a specialized finite-dimensional reduction would be needed for robust automated iteration.
